item.title: Big O Notation Explained, why is it important?
item.author: Lukas Van der Spiegel
item.content:encoded:
Big O Notation is important because it helps analyze the efficiency of algorithms. - geeksforgeeks.orgWhen writing code, efficiency matters. Big O Notation helps developers understand how algorithms perform as input size grows. Whether you're sorting data, searching through a list, or optimizing performance, knowing the common Big O complexities—like O(1), O(n), O(log n), and O(n²)—is essential. In this post, we’ll break down these notations, explain their significance, and show you why understanding algorithm complexity can help you write better, faster, and more scalable code.Big O Notation is a mathematical concept used to analyze the efficiency of algorithms. It helps developers understand how time and space complexity scale as input size grows. By mastering Big O, you can make better decisions for performance optimization and write more scalable code.Helps in selecting the most efficient algorithms.Improves scalability and performance.Essential knowledge for coding interviews.Prevents performance bottlenecks in large-scale applications.Let's ask chatgpt the most common big O complexities:Example: Accessing an element in an array by index (arr[i]).Performance remains the same no matter how large the input is.Fastest and most desirable complexity.
2. O(log n) – Logarithmic Time Input size shrinks significantly with each step.Efficient for large datasets.Example: Looping through an array (for loop over n elements).Performance degrades proportionally as the input grows.Common in search algorithms like linear search.
4. O(n log n) – Linearithmic Time Example: Efficient sorting algorithms (Merge Sort, QuickSort).Slightly worse than O(n), but significantly better than O(n²) for large data.Used when sorting large datasets efficiently.
5. O(n²) – Quadratic Time Example: Nested loops (e.g., Bubble Sort, Selection Sort).Becomes very slow as n grows.Often a sign of inefficient algorithms.
6. O(2ⁿ) – Exponential Time Example: Recursive Fibonacci algorithm.Growth doubles with each new input.Practically infeasible for large n.
7. O(n!) – Factorial Time Example: Solving the traveling salesman problem via brute force.Worst possible time complexity.Used in combinatorial problems, but avoided in real applications.
Let's break it down in a use cases ? Use Binary Search (O(log n)) instead of Linear Search (O(n)).? Use Merge Sort (O(n log n)) instead of Bubble Sort (O(n²)).? Use Hash Tables (O(1) for average case).Every algorithm has a best, average, and worst-case scenario. The same algorithm does not necessarily always perform with the same efficiency. Many influential factors in a computer, such as hardware, memory management, and system load, can impact execution time. Understanding these variations helps in selecting the right algorithm for different situations.Quick generated with AI (Python)We see 2 search algorithms compared at runtime.Big O Notation is a crucial tool for evaluating algorithm efficiency in terms of time and space complexity. It helps developers understand how their code scales as input size increases, enabling them to choose the most optimal solutions. By recognizing common complexities like O(1), O(n), and O(n²), you can write faster, more efficient programs and avoid performance bottlenecks. Mastering Big O is essential for improving scalability, optimizing applications, and excelling in coding interviews. 🚀 This is written as a quick summary of my research (school assignment), based everything on that and briefly went over this topic. Certain information has been observed from academical papers, wikipedia and geeksforgeeks websites. Most of all is rewritten in my words, with correction from AI.

item.title: The Ultimate Guide to Chart Types in Power BI
item.author: Kaira Kelvin.
item.content:encoded:
Data Visualization focuses on presenting data in charts and graphs. The challenge is to get the art right without getting the science wrong, and vice versa. Data Storytelling involves structuring data into a meaningful narrative that guides toward a conclusion.Elements of a good data story.Data - Well prepared, accurate, and relevant information.Visuals- Charts and graphs that enhance dashboards.Narrative - A compelling storyline that connects insights with business impact.Data preparation and modeling in Power BIImport the data in Power BI - CSV, excel, SQL, webData Cleaning and Transformation -Power Query for data cleaning -Handle missing values, duplicates, and errors - before visualization.Build a data model. -Schema - Star schema -is simpler and faster. -Snowflake schema - Define relationships between tables -one to many, many to many, one to one.DAX for storytelling. -Use measure and calculated columns -use this properly -Common dax formulas -Sum(),Average(),Calculate()Designing effective Power BI Dashboards.Dashboards vs Report. a. Dashboard - interactive visualization that provides a high- level view of key metrics and insights. b. Report - a report designed to be printed or exported with a fixed layout, unlike interactive dashboards.Principles of Dashboard Design.Below are the principles for a good dashboard.Know your audience - always ask yourself this question before designing your dashboard. > Who is going to consume the dashboard?Think About The Flow of Data And Power BI Dashboard Layout.Which graph or KPI should I represent first?Is the dashboard too cluttered?How do I prioritize information?How can users drill down into specific data points?What level of interaction provides the most value? 3. a. Clarity & Simplicity - Avoid Clutter. b. Visual Hierarchy - Also prioritize important information. c. Use of colors & themes - Keep it professional and readable.Visualization is worth billions or trillions of data items.
Why use data visualizations? Visualizations help us understand complex data. The best reason to use a visualization to understand your data is that most data sets are far too large to consume in their raw format. Humans are limited in what information we process and compare in our heads, especially if that information resides in a million-row data set, since human beings are good at processing visual information. A data visualization has to be accurate thus conveying the data. It must not mislead or distort. Some pre-attentive attributes are better at showing quantitative (Measured) data and some are better at showing qualitative (categorical) data.Chart types and their uses.
1. Charts that show trends They are used to visualize a measure over time to see how that measure changes from point to point. line chart views trends in data over time, years, days, months, quarters, and downtrends. stock price change over five years website page views during a month.Capture multiple numeric variables over time. It can include multiple axes allowing comparison of different units. The secondary axis is often used in Power BI. Apple vs Amazon stocks over time. Just like line charts, area charts are used to visualize trends over time. Area charts, allow us to visualize comparisons across multiple categories showing how each category contributes to the overall measure. They do this by filling in each area of the chart with their unique color as demonstrated in the visual on the left. Area chart - Use for rates rather than totals. Use sensible base geography -internet usage rates in certain geographies, -house prices in different neighborhoods.Mostly used for variation of area charts. This is best used to track the breakdown of a numeric value by subgroups. Multiple data series are layered on top of one another, which is useful for understanding part-to-whole relationships.It means _sehemu hadi jumla _ in swahili. A comparison between a part and a whole of one thing and how it is similar to a part and a whole of another thing.a). Pie chart & Donut chart.(i). Pie chart - A circular chart that could present values of a dataset in the form of circle slices. (ii). Donut charts are a way in which to represent how data is spread across different categorical values. Donut charts are much the same as pie charts but with a hole in the middle. These are 2d rectangles whose size is proportional to the value being measured and can be used to display hierarchically structured data. Method for displaying hierarchical data using nested figures ,usually rectangles. They require one or more dimensions and one or two measures to build them. Treemap - Show hierarchical data as a proportion of a whole. Storage usage across computer machines comparing fiscal budgets between years. How can you show the part-to-whole relationship between marital status vs Distance from home in (km)Drag the sum distance in (km) to the size mark card and marital status to the color To Distinguish between the different squares, add labels . Drag sum distance in (km) and marital status to the label shelf. The order you drag the fields onto the marks card depends on whether you want sum distance in (km) or marital status to appear on the label first.Moreso u can edit the labels manually. Click labels on the Tableau shelf and the following window will be displayed.Select the three dots next to the Text option,you can specifically edit what appears on the label.A treemap can be used to display part-to-whole or hierarchical relationships using rectangles or squares as the area of the measure. However, the user could take longer to interpret this chart type over a bar chart, because the human eye can interpret the pre-attentive attribute of length better than the size attribute of an area. A treemap orders the segments based on highest to lowest with the largest/highest sections being in the top-right area.To show how the subcategories fit into these categories, all we need to do is click the plus sign on Marital Status or sort them which will drill down to Sub-Category.The advantage of using treemaps is you can use multiple measures. I don’t recommend sizing by a measure that has negative numbers, because to show the subcategory, you would have to use absolute values, which could be misleading. Show the relationship between two factors. Segment analysis of target market or sales leads by individual rep. Display a single month of a calendar to show the number of orders shipped by day. To start with, filter to a single month by dragging the hire Date to filters, selecting Month/Year, and choosing your month.A calendar heatmap shows you hot and cold spots in a monthly view. The decomposition tree visual in Power BI lets you visualize data across multiple dimensions.They are easy to read and explain. They are fantastic way to dig into drivers of a specific metric. It is valuable for ad hoc exploration ,conducting root cause analysis and identifying influential variables which explain the variation in the target variable. The architecture looks like branches of a tree, hence decomposition TREE. It requires two types of inputs .Analyze – the metric you would like to analyze. It must be a measure or an aggregate.Explain By – one or more dimensions you would like to drill down into. Decomposition trees are a great method to analyze a target variable.They can be complimented with other visuals or information to build further context (e.g cards ).Like for instance you can get the total average working years of women in sales executive department who are mid in their career stage and job level 3. Below is the illustration, so the card shows the average working years.3.Visualize a single value - is great for showing and tracking Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in dashboards or presentations. - best to be used on small datasets, usually displays tabular data in a table. -Gauge chart looks similar to a speedometer of a Car. used to measure and gives a visual display of the amount,level or contents of something. Often used in executive dashboards to show relevant KPIs. A gauge chart is a half-circle chart, which tells whether a target is achieved or not. Gauge charts are generally used to measure the progress toward a goal.Distributions - describes how values are distributed for a field,simply terms which values are common and which are not. Unlike a bar chart, it shows the distribution and frequency of data converts numerical data into bins as columns. You can change the bin size in the histogram. This type of chart shows the distribution of your data using equal bin sizes. The bin sizes can be static, as the solution provided, or you can use a parameter to let the user choose the bin size. Bins/class each bar height indicates the frequency of data points with a value within the corresponding bin. They Show the distribution of a set of the data, Examples Understanding your data at a glance seeing how data is skewed towards one end, and identifying outliers in your data.*. shows full distributions of the data alongside summary statistics.* used to show the relationship between two things to understand if a relationship exists. Scatterplot - Investigate relationships between quantitative values examples Male versus female likelihood of having lung cancer at different ages or technology early adopters and laggards purchase patterns of smartphones. Scatter plots are my favorite chart type because of their ability to reveal and communicate correlations. Bar charts are one of the most popular and effective charts you can use. They allow us to compare measures over different categorical data values easily. It allows the human eye to compare differences between length or height, one of the preattentive attributes. There are two types of basic bar charts: 2. Vertical. Bar charts have other variations, including stacked bar charts, diverging bar charts, and histograms. Drawbacks of using a vertical bar chart - the reader would have to tilt their head to read the headers.The benefit of using a Horizontal bar chart - it allows the sub-category header to have more space. It allows the reader not to shift position to read therefore allowing faster interpretation of the chart. Bar charts are great for comparing whether a subcategory is above or below a A constant line is used for charts when you have a specific target or reference point. Bar - Compare data across categories. eg volume of shirts in different sizes, or percent of spending by department. a.The length of the bars tells you which category has the highest value. b.The axis of the bar chart indicates what that value represents. Stacked bar charts are useful for showing potential relationships in data and display an extra level of detail. To enhance this chart type you can add labels to each gender and a total per subcategory.100 % Stacked Bar Chart(Percent of Total). Stacked bar charts are useful for showing the contributions of each dimension of data, which is known as part-to-whole relationships. The drawback, however, is that 100% stacked bar charts don’t allow for a comparison across the subcategories for each gender.100% Stacked bar charts are ideal for comparing segments within each bar. In our example the Percent of Total allows the user to see the percentage of each gender regardless of the total value for that subcategory. When you see an upward triangle on a green field that means the field has been changed to a Table Calculation. You might have a data set that has multiple measures in different columns. A shared axis will allow you to compare two or more measures on one axis. This is especially beneficial if the measures are on a similar scale.Maps are a good way to visualize location-based data. Tableau recognizes geographical fields such as country and city and automatically generates latitudes and longitude. This prevents the user from manually looking up coordinates or paying extra for this type of data. Maps provide an effective and intuitive way to represent geographical data. Density maps, visualize with intensity where there are larger clusters of data points to make it obvious where the most activity is taking place. An important feature of density maps isn't just the areas of large clusters but also outliers. The Density mark shows where the highest levels of concentration are within your scatter plot When using filled maps, you should use them with caution. Since our eyes are naturally drawn to larger areas on the map.Therefore, when you have smaller countries or states that might have a higher value, their smaller size means you might not instantly see that. This is where a symbol map is better at visualizing the smaller areas. The Highlight table is a very easy and effective visualization to build. A highlight table uses diverging colors to demonstrate high and low numerical values across dimensions. Highlight Table - shows detailed information on heat maps, eg the percent of a market for different segments or sales numbers in a region.Gant charts provide a visual representation of schedules, tasks, and timelines which can be effective in project management and planning.Gantt - shows duration over time eg project timeline duration of a machine's use availability of players on a team. It enables users to track progress, identify dependencies, and monitor the length of activities. calculate the difference in date using the function in tableau known as DATEDIFFGantt has many use cases, and one of those is to build a barcode chart. Barcodes are used to see data and can be read by a machine.Bar charts are heavily used in data analytics. The soundwave chart is a version of the bar chart. It uses the standard bar chart approach. The soundwave chart can be used to enhance the visualization of the volume. Soundwave charts are great for showing volume. This type of chart doubles the extreme values.

item.title: Building a Robust Message Queue System with Kafka and GoFrame
item.author: Jones Charles
item.content:encoded:
Hey fellow devs! 👋 Today, we're diving into how to build a robust message queue system using Apache Kafka with the GoFrame framework. Whether you're handling high-throughput data streams, building event-driven architectures, or just want to decouple your services, this guide has got you covered!We'll create a complete message queue system that can:Set up Kafka producers and consumers in GoFrameHandle message publishing and consumptionImplement robust error handlingAdd retry mechanisms for failed operationsBefore we start, make sure you have:Go installed on your machineBasic understanding of Go and message queuesKafka and ZooKeeper running locally or a Kafka cluster you can connect toGoFrame framework installedIf you're new to Kafka, here's what you need to know. Kafka is a distributed messaging system that excels at handling high-throughput data streams. The key concepts are:: Your message sender: Your message receiver: Categories for your messages: How topics are split for scalability
1. First, Install the Kafka Client go get github.com/IBM/sarama
2. Set Up Your Configuration Create a config file that Kafka will use:
Making It Production-Ready 🚀
Sending Messages with Error Handling
Consuming Messages Like a Pro Here's a robust consumer implementation with error handling and graceful shutdown:Here's a robust retry mechanism for handling message processing failures:: Always ensure ZooKeeper is running before starting Kafka: ./zookeeper-server-start.sh config/zookeeper.properties
./kafka-server-start.sh config/server.properties : Keep track of consumer lag to ensure your system is keeping up with message flow.: Always close your producers and consumers properly:Now that you have a solid foundation, you might want to explore:Setting up message compression for better performanceImplementing dead letter queues for failed messagesAdding metrics and monitoringSetting up multiple consumer groupsWe've built a robust message queue system using Kafka and GoFrame! The combination provides a scalable, reliable solution for handling high-throughput message processing.Have you implemented Kafka in your Go projects? What challenges did you face? Share your experiences in the comments below! 👇Found this helpful? Follow me for more Go tutorials and don't forget to ❤️ this post!

item.title: How to be Test Driven with Spark: Chapter 4 - Leaning into Property Based Testing
item.author: Nicoda-27
item.content:encoded: This goal of this tutorial is to provide a way to easily be test driven with spark on your local setup without using cloud resources.This is a series of tutorials and the initial chapters can be found in:The test that you implemented in Chapter 3 is great, yet not complete as it takes only a limited amount of data. As spark is used to process data at scale, you have to test at scale too.There are several solutions, the first one being taking a snapshot of production data and reusing at the test level (meaning integration test or local test). The second one is to generate synthetic data based on the data schema. With the second approach, you will be leaning into a property based testing approach.The second approach will be leveraged here as the test case generation is deported to automated generation.The python ecosystem provides for proper property based testing, or for fake data generation. is way more powerful than Faker in the sense that it will generate test cases for you based on data property (being a string, being an integer etc) and shrink the test cases when unexpected behavior happen. will be used here to generate synthetic data based on business property.You need two new fixtures similar to and that will generate synthetic data. First you need to install faker as a dev dependency:In the above, a data frame of 100 000 rows is generated, feel free to increase the size to generate larger data frames. Fake names, surnames and date are generated on the fly according to business needs.You can also create in , there is a dependency on from that needs to be handled:The is reused from a sample of and job name are generated on the fly.The test can now be created:And you can launch pytest -k test_transfo_w_synthetic_data -s that should pass.You might notice that test_transfo_w_synthetic_data is a bit slow, indeed it's generating a decent amount of data (even though far from a big data scale), modifying the data frames and joining two together.In a test driven approach, it's necessary to have a quick feedback loop to iterate quickly on your local setup. Yet, this tests needs to be launched anyway as they validate behavior with decent amount of data.A solution is to add tags to tests like so:This tag can be leveraged by pytest to filter out tests at execution time, see documentation. is now aware of this new marker when launching:It will validate only the tests not marked as slow.In the ci, there is nothing to change as by default will launch all the test.On the next chapter, the next chapter will focus on test repeatability by improving how java is used for at the test level.You can find the original materials in spark_tdd. This repository exposes what's the expected repository layout at the end of each chapter in each branch:

item.title: How to get a gene sequence from entrez using biopython?
item.author: Steven Walt Li
item.content:encoded:
To retrieve a gene sequence from the NCBI Entrez database using Biopython, you can use the Entrez module from the Bio package. Below is a step-by-step guide on how to do this:
Step 1: Install Biopython If you haven't already installed Biopython, you can do so using pip:
Step 2: Import Necessary Modules You need to import the Entrez module from Biopython.NCBI requires you to provide an email address when using their Entrez services.Entrez.email = "your_email@example.com" # Replace with your email
Step 4: Search for the Gene Use the Entrez.esearch function to search for the gene of interest. For example, let's search for the human insulin gene (INS).search_term = "INS[Gene Name] AND Homo sapiens[Organism]" handle = Entrez.esearch(db="nucleotide", term=search_term) record = Entrez.read(handle) handle.close()
Step 5: Retrieve the Gene Sequence Once you have the list of IDs from the search, you can use Entrez.efetch to retrieve the sequence.gene_id = record["IdList"][0] # Get the first ID from the search results handle = Entrez.efetch(db="nucleotide", id=gene_id, rettype="fasta", retmode="text") gene_sequence = handle.read() handle.close()
Step 6: Print or Save the Sequence You can now print or save the gene sequence.print(gene_sequence)
# Optionally, save to a file with open("gene_sequence.fasta", "w") as file: file.write(gene_sequence) Here is the complete code:from Bio import Entrez
# Set your email Entrez.email = "your_email@example.com"
# Search for the gene search_term = "INS[Gene Name] AND Homo sapiens[Organism]" handle = Entrez.esearch(db="nucleotide", term=search_term) record = Entrez.read(handle) handle.close()
# Retrieve the gene sequence gene_id = record["IdList"][0] # Get the first ID from the search results handle = Entrez.efetch(db="nucleotide", id=gene_id, rettype="fasta", retmode="text") gene_sequence = handle.read() handle.close()
# Print or save the sequence print(gene_sequence)
# Optionally, save to a file with open("gene_sequence.fasta", "w") as file: file.write(gene_sequence) The db parameter in Entrez.esearch and Entrez.efetch can be set to different databases like nucleotide, protein, gene, etc., depending on what you are looking for. Be mindful of NCBI's rate limits. If you are making many requests, consider using Entrez.sleep to pause between requests.This code will fetch the gene sequence in FASTA format and either print it or save it to a file.

item.title: Open-Source ETL to prepare data for RAG 🦀 🐍
item.author: Linghua
item.content:encoded: I’ve built an open source ETL framework (CocoIndex) to prepare data for RAG with my friend. Support custom logic - you can plugin your own choice of chunking, embedding, vector stores; plugin your own logic like lego. We have three examples in the repo for now. In the long run, we also want to support dedupe, reconcile etc.Incremental updates. We provide state management out-of-box to minimize re-computation. Right now, it checks if a file from a data source is updated. In future, it will be at smaller granularity, e.g., at chunk level. Python SDK (RUST core 🦀 with Python binding 🐍)🔗 GitHub Repo: CocoIndex - Appreciate your support with a github star ⭐ !Sincerely looking for feedback and learning from your thoughts. Would love contributors too if you are interested :) Thank you so much!

item.title: My First Rust Lesson: String vs &str
item.author: tomomomomo
item.content:encoded: Today I’ve learned the difference between &str and String. String is a wrapper of Vec, with capacity, len, and ptr. &str is a reference to an existing string. It’s unchangeable. To change it, you need a new String with a new heap area. If I want to make a Web app, there are so many places to use String. But when I want to make an AI model, I might use &str more often, I think.When I was using Laravel, I didn’t recognize the existence of heap and stack. The two seem very interesting and attractive.Yesterday, a very kind person told me about Rust’s two string types. So I read about them in the book. Thank you!

item.title: Web Scraping with Selenium and Python: A Developer’s Guide
item.author: Alex Aslam
item.content:encoded: In the realm of web scraping, stands out as a versatile tool for automating browsers, making it indispensable for extracting data from dynamic, JavaScript-heavy websites. While libraries like Beautiful Soup and Scrapy excel with static pages, Selenium mimics human interaction, enabling developers to scrape complex SPAs (Single-Page Applications), handle logins, and navigate AJAX-driven content. This guide dives into using Selenium with Python, offering code examples, advanced techniques, and best practices tailored for developers.: Interact with pages that load data via JavaScript (e.g., React, Angular). : Automate clicks, form submissions, and scrolling. : Control Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and more. Testing & Scraping Hybrid Use Cases: Validate UI while extracting data. : Use tools like Scrapy or Requests-HTML for static sites, but choose Selenium when JavaScript execution is critical.2. Install Browser DriversSelenium requires a driver to interface with your browser. Popular options: : Use to auto-download drivers:pip webdriver-manager Basic Web Scraping WorkflowUse CSS selectors, XPath, or IDs:Avoid by waiting for elements to load:3. Click Buttons/Pagination1. Handle Logins & CookiesSpeed up execution by running browsers in the background:Use proxies to prevent IP bans. Add randomized delays between requests:: Prefer CSS selectors or XPath over slower methods like . : Always close browsers to free resources: E-commerce Price Tracking: Monitor Amazon, eBay, or Shopify stores. : Extract public posts from Twitter or Instagram (avoid private data!). : Aggregate listings from Indeed or LinkedIn. : Scrape authenticated analytics tools like Google Analytics. Selenium vs. Playwright/PuppeteerCross-browser, mature, Python-nativeSlower, requires driver setupFaster, built-in waits, multi-browser supportOptimized for Chrome/ChromiumNode.js-centric (Pyppeteer is unofficial)Legacy browser support (e.g., IE). Integration with Python testing frameworks (e.g., PyTest). Troubleshooting Common IssuesElementNotInteractableException: Use explicit waits or scroll to the element. StaleElementReferenceException: Re-find elements after page reloads. : Not solvable via Selenium—use third-party services or avoid triggering them. : Check rules at https://website.com/robots.txt. : Avoid overloading servers. : Scrape only publicly available data. Selenium with Python empowers developers to scrape even the most complex websites by automating real user interactions. While it demands more resources than static scrapers, its flexibility in handling JavaScript and dynamic content is unmatched. Integrate with Beautiful Soup for hybrid static/dynamic scraping. Experiment with parallel scraping using threading. : Pair Selenium with proxies and headless mode for scalable, stealthy scraping. Always stay compliant with website policies!

item.title: How Rust Handles Closures: Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce
item.author: Leapcell
item.content:encoded:
In the Rust programming language, closures are a powerful and flexible feature that allows you to define anonymous functions and capture variables from their surrounding environment. Rust’s closure system is defined by three core traits—Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce—that determine how closures interact with captured variables, how many times they can be called, and whether they can modify their environment. Understanding these traits is crucial for mastering Rust’s closure mechanism and writing efficient, safe code.This article provides a detailed introduction to the three traits—Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce—including their definitions, use cases, methods, applicable scenarios, and best practices, along with code examples to help you comprehensively learn the relevant concepts.
What Are Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce? Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce are three traits defined in Rust’s standard library to describe the behavior of closures (or any callable objects). Their primary difference lies in how they access captured variables and the ownership rules when they are called:: Indicates that a closure can be called once. After being called, the closure itself is consumed and can no longer be used.: Indicates that a closure can be called multiple times and can modify captured variables when invoked.: Indicates that a closure can be called multiple times and only reads captured variables without modifying them.There is an inheritance relationship among these three traits:Fn inherits from FnMut, and FnMut inherits from FnOnce.Therefore, if a closure implements Fn, it also automatically implements FnMut and FnOnce; if it implements FnMut, it also implements FnOnce.The trait defines a method with the following signature:: takes (instead of or ), meaning that when the closure is called, it transfers ownership of itself and can therefore only be invoked once.: Suitable for scenarios where a closure needs to move captured variables or perform a one-time operation.The trait defines a method with the following signature:: takes , allowing the closure to modify its internal state or captured variables when invoked, and it can be called multiple times.: Suitable for scenarios where a closure needs to modify its environment across multiple calls.The trait defines a method with the following signature:: takes , meaning that the closure only immutably borrows itself and the captured variables. It can be called multiple times without modifying the environment.: Suitable for scenarios where a closure needs to be called multiple times and only reads data.
How Closures Implement These Traits Rust’s compiler automatically determines which traits a closure implements based on how it uses captured variables. A closure can capture variables in three ways:: The closure takes ownership of the variable.By mutable reference (): The closure captures a mutable reference to the variable.By immutable reference (): The closure captures an immutable reference to the variable.The implemented trait depends on how the captured variable is used:: The closure moves the captured variable.Implements FnMut and FnOnce: The closure modifies the captured variable.Implements Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce: The closure only reads the captured variable.
Closure Implementing FnOnce : The closure captures by move, taking ownership of it and dropping it when called. Since is moved, the closure can only be called once, making it implement only .
Closure Implementing FnMut : The closure captures by mutable reference and modifies it with each call. Since it needs to modify its environment, it implements and .: The closure captures by immutable reference and only reads it without modification. Therefore, it implements , , and .
Using These Traits in Function Parameters Closures can be passed as arguments to functions, and functions need to specify the required closure behavior using trait bounds.: accepts an closure and calls it once, making it suitable for closures that move captured variables.: accepts an closure and calls it twice. The closure can modify the captured variable. Note that must be declared as .: accepts an closure and calls it twice. The closure only reads the captured variable.Choosing the right trait depends on the behavior required for the closure:: The closure is called only once or needs to move captured variables.: A one-time operation that transfers ownership.: The closure needs to be called multiple times and modify captured variables.: A counter or state update.: The closure needs to be called multiple times and only reads captured variables.: Logging or data queries.When designing functions, selecting the most permissive trait increases flexibility. For example, accepts all closures but limits invocation, while allows multiple calls but requires immutability.: If a closure doesn’t need to modify variables, use for maximum compatibility.Use FnMut when modification is needed: Choose when a closure needs to update state.Use FnOnce for single-use closures: If a closure moves variables or performs a one-time task, use .Choose the right trait for APIs: Use for single-use calls, for multiple read-only calls, and for multiple calls with modifications.: Ensure captured variables live long enough to avoid borrow errors.Leapcell is the Next-Gen Serverless Platform for Web Hosting, Async Tasks, and Redis:Develop with Node.js, Python, Go, or Rust.Deploy unlimited projects for freepay only for usage — no requests, no charges.Unbeatable Cost EfficiencyPay-as-you-go with no idle charges.Example: $25 supports 6.94M requests at a 60ms average response time.Streamlined Developer ExperienceIntuitive UI for effortless setup.Fully automated CI/CD pipelines and GitOps integration.Real-time metrics and logging for actionable insights.Effortless Scalability and High PerformanceAuto-scaling to handle high concurrency with ease.Zero operational overhead — just focus on building.

item.title: Master Automation with These Python Hacks
item.author: Resource Bunk
item.content:encoded:
Automation isn't just a tool—it's a lifestyle. Your scripts should work as hard as you do, making tedious tasks vanish in the blink of an eye.In this guide, we’re going to explore a collection of lesser-known Python tips and tricks designed to boost your automation projects. Whether you're streamlining data processing, fine-tuning your error handling, or leveraging decorators to keep your code clean, this article is packed with actionable insights, code snippets, stats, and valuable resources. And if you're hungry for more resources and community support, don’t forget to check out Python Developer Resources - Made by 0x3d.site for a curated hub featuring essential tools, articles, and trending discussions.
1. Optimizing Script Performance When your automation tasks start to scale, every millisecond counts. Instead of waiting for your scripts to lag, adopt these optimization strategies to keep your projects lightning-fast.
A. Efficient Data Handling Handling data efficiently is crucial. A few techniques can make a huge difference:List Comprehensions vs. Traditional Loops: List comprehensions are not only succinct but often faster than traditional loops. Compare these two approaches: "List comprehensions offer a cleaner syntax and improved performance for simple operations." Generators for Memory Efficiency: When working with large data sets, generators can help save memory by producing items only when required. Leveraging Built-In Libraries: Libraries like NumPy are optimized for numerical operations and can significantly speed up processing. For further tips and tutorials, visit Developer Resources.
B. Profiling and Benchmarking Before diving into optimizations, it’s essential to identify the slow parts of your code. Python’s built-in module is perfect for this: Recent benchmarks indicate that list comprehensions can be up to 30% faster than traditional loops for small to medium-sized lists. Adopting efficient data structures can lead to noticeable performance improvements in real-world automation tasks. "Profiling is the first step to meaningful optimization. Without knowing where the bottleneck lies, you’re just guessing." For more advanced profiling techniques and performance tips, be sure to explore the Articles section on our website.
2. Effective Error Handling Robust error handling is the backbone of any resilient automation script. It ensures that your code doesn't crumble under unexpected conditions and continues running smoothly.Use the try-except blocks wisely by catching specific errors rather than a generic exception:This approach prevents masking unexpected issues and makes debugging easier.
B. Resource Cleanup with Finally When working with external resources like files or network connections, the block ensures that clean-up actions are always performed: "Reliable error handling isn’t about preventing errors; it’s about managing them gracefully so that your automation keeps running." Learn more about effective error handling and best practices by visiting the Trending Discussions section on our website.
3. Using Decorators for Code Efficiency Decorators are a powerful feature in Python that allow you to extend the behavior of a function without modifying its code. They help in keeping your code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) and are perfect for repetitive tasks like logging, caching, or even access control.
A. Understanding Decorators At their core, decorators are functions that wrap other functions. Here’s a simple example: "Decorators allow you to inject code before and after function calls, making them ideal for cross-cutting concerns like logging and performance monitoring." Automatically log every call to a function:
Use caching to avoid repeated heavy computations. Python’s built-in is a great tool: Once you’ve mastered the basics, it’s time to explore more advanced strategies to enhance your automation scripts even further.
A. Asynchronous Programming Python’s asynchronous programming features, available through the library, allow your scripts to handle I/O-bound tasks efficiently without blocking. "Asynchronous programming can significantly improve the efficiency of your automation, especially when dealing with network operations or file I/O."
B. Unit Testing and Debugging No automation project is complete without rigorous testing. Python’s framework and debugging tools like help you maintain high-quality, error-free code. "Regular testing not only prevents bugs but also boosts your confidence as you add new features to your automation scripts."
5. Real-World Automation Stats and Success Stories Automation in Python isn’t just theory—it’s a practice that’s reshaping industries: Companies report up to a 50% reduction in manual work when automating routine tasks. Over 70% of tech enterprises now rely on Python for their automation needs. The Python community has grown exponentially, with countless open-source projects and libraries enhancing productivity every day. "In the race to optimize workflows, Python stands out as the go-to language for automation—combining ease of use with immense power." These stats underscore why investing time in learning and applying these hacks can have a transformative impact on your projects. For more inspiring success stories and community insights, be sure to visit Python Developer Resources.
Conclusion: Empower Your Automation Journey Automation is your ticket to a more efficient, stress-free coding life. With these Python hacks, you’re equipped to optimize performance, handle errors gracefully, and write cleaner, more efficient code. Each tip—from leveraging generators and list comprehensions to using decorators and asynchronous programming—adds another tool to your developer toolkit.Remember, every challenge in coding is an opportunity to learn and grow. Embrace these strategies, experiment with them in your projects, and watch as your automation scripts evolve into robust, reliable systems.Take charge, experiment boldly, and let Python do the heavy lifting. Happy coding and remember: your next breakthrough is just a hack away!

item.title: Serverless Python APIs: Hype or Future?
item.author: Snappy Tuts
item.content:encoded:
Should You Ditch Traditional API Hosting in 2025?Imagine a world where you can focus solely on writing Python code without the constant worry of managing servers or troubleshooting infrastructure issues. Picture your development process streamlined to let your creativity and problem-solving skills shine. In 2025, the debate is real: Are serverless Python APIs a revolutionary step forward or just another fleeting trend? This in-depth article will guide you through comparing AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, and Azure Functions for Python APIs, reveal why some developers are returning to traditional servers, and provide a practical step-by-step guide to convert your Flask API to a serverless architecture. info: "Serverless computing is transforming backend development—it's not just a trend but a shift in how we build scalable, resilient applications."
1. What Exactly Is Serverless? Serverless computing doesn’t mean the absence of servers—it means you no longer need to manage them. The cloud provider handles scaling, patching, and maintenance while you focus solely on your code. Your function runs only when needed, so you pay only for the compute time you consume. However, this convenience comes with trade-offs like cold start delays and cost unpredictability. The system automatically adjusts to incoming traffic. No need to manage hardware or server maintenance.Cost-efficiency (in theory): Pay for what you use rather than keeping a server running 24/7.info: "In a survey of 500+ developers, 62% expressed excitement over the reduced operational overhead offered by serverless architectures."
2. Comparing the Big Players: AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, and Azure Functions When choosing a serverless platform, it’s essential to understand the strengths and weaknesses of the major providers. Let’s break down how AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, and Azure Functions stack up for running Python APIs. Automatically scales with traffic. Seamless integration with a multitude of AWS services. Extensive community support and rich documentation. Infrequently used functions can experience delays. Costs can spike with unpredictable traffic patterns.Code Example – Simple AWS Lambda Function:info: "AWS Lambda has seen a steady increase in usage, with many organizations reporting up to a 40% reduction in operational overhead." Simple interface and straightforward deployment. Generally provides fast response times. Works well with other Google Cloud services. Similar to AWS, you may experience delays. Might face performance bottlenecks under extreme loads.Code Example – Google Cloud Function:info: "Developers appreciate Google Cloud Functions for its ease of setup and integration with existing Google services."For additional comparisons and community feedback, check out on Python Developer Resources. Great for hybrid cloud environments. Excellent integration with Visual Studio Code. Strong support for debugging and monitoring. Although improvements are ongoing, delays still occur.Platform-Specific Nuances: May require more configuration to optimize performance.Code Example – Azure Function for Python:info: "Azure Functions continue to gain traction in enterprise environments, offering flexibility and integration with legacy systems."
3. The Hidden Costs: Why Some Developers Return to Traditional Servers Despite the buzz, serverless computing isn’t a silver bullet. Many developers are weighing the hidden costs and performance challenges that sometimes drive them back to traditional server hosting.While serverless billing is based on actual usage, spikes in traffic can lead to unexpected bills. For applications with unpredictable loads, the fixed cost of a dedicated server might offer better budgeting control.info: "A recent study indicated that while serverless can reduce costs by 20% under steady loads, unpredictable spikes can increase costs by up to 50% compared to traditional hosting."A "cold start" happens when a function is invoked after a period of inactivity, causing a delay as the function is loaded. This latency can affect user experience, particularly in time-sensitive applications. Schedule periodic invocations to avoid cold starts.Optimize Code Initialization: Minimize the amount of code that runs during the function’s startup. of developers report cold starts as a significant challenge. have adopted strategies to keep functions warm, according to recent surveys.
4. Converting a Flask API to Serverless: A Practical Step-by-Step Guide Ready to take the plunge? Converting your existing Flask API to a serverless function might seem daunting, but with careful planning and the right tools, it’s entirely achievable. Here’s how to do it.
Step 1: Assess Your Flask API Begin by reviewing your Flask application. Identify the endpoints, middleware, and dependencies. Evaluate if the function-level granularity of serverless fits your usage patterns.info: "Start small: Convert a single endpoint first to see how it behaves in a serverless environment before fully migrating."
Step 2: Choose Your Serverless Provider Select the cloud provider that best aligns with your needs—whether it's AWS, Google, or Azure. Consider your current ecosystem, expected traffic, and potential cold start impacts.
Step 3: Refactor Your Code for Serverless Serverless functions are designed to be stateless. Remove any code that assumes a persistent server environment and externalize state management (e.g., databases, caches).Using Zappa for AWS Lambda: Install Zappa, initialize your project, and deploy:pip zappa zappa init zappa deploy dev For more detailed guides on converting Flask apps, visit on Python Developer Resources.Deploy your refactored code using the CLI tools provided by your chosen cloud service. Test each endpoint extensively, checking for cold start delays and performance bottlenecks. Use AWS CloudWatch, Google Cloud Monitoring, or Azure Monitor to track function performance:info: "Monitoring is critical. Use cloud-native tools to gain insights into performance and optimize accordingly."
Step 5: Optimize Continuously Regularly review performance metrics and cost reports. Adjust memory allocation, timeout settings, and keep-alive strategies to ensure a balance between performance and cost.
5. Real-World Stats and Success Stories Statistics and success stories bring theory to life. Here are a few compelling numbers and real-world insights: The serverless market is projected to grow at a over the next five years. This rapid adoption is driven by the promise of reduced operational overhead and scalability benefits. of new cloud-native projects in 2024 are considering a serverless-first approach. info: "Adopting serverless architecture has allowed teams to focus more on core business logic rather than infrastructure management." Traditional hosting can sometimes be during unpredictable traffic spikes when compared to well-optimized serverless functions.
6. Addressing Common Concerns and Overcoming Challenges No technology is without its challenges. Serverless functions may introduce cold start delays and hidden costs. However, many teams have mitigated these issues through smart design and proactive monitoring.info: "Reliability comes from understanding the trade-offs. Start with non-critical endpoints and gradually transition your workload to serverless."Security remains a shared responsibility. While cloud providers secure the underlying infrastructure, you must secure your application code. Follow best practices: validate inputs, enforce strict authentication, and keep dependencies updated.Since serverless functions are stateless, move your session data and persistent state to external services such as managed databases (e.g., AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL) or caching solutions like Redis.For deeper dives into securing your applications, explore the curated guides on .
7. Weighing the Future: Serverless vs. Traditional Hosting The decision between serverless and traditional hosting isn’t black and white. Consider these factors: Serverless offers seamless scaling, but traditional servers provide granular control over performance. Fixed costs of traditional hosting simplify budgeting, whereas serverless may lead to variable bills under fluctuating loads. Traditional hosting demands continuous maintenance, while serverless offloads much of that burden to the cloud provider.info: "For many developers, the future lies in a hybrid approach—leveraging the best of both serverless and traditional hosting to meet specific needs."For a wealth of comparative studies and real-world case studies, visit on Python Developer Resources.
8. Bringing It All Together: Your Path Forward Serverless Python APIs are reshaping the landscape of backend development. Whether you choose AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, or Azure Functions, the critical part is understanding the benefits, trade-offs, and best practices. Here’s your action plan: Experiment with converting a single endpoint from your Flask API to serverless. Test performance, monitor costs, and refine your approach.Leverage Community Resources: Join discussions, check trending repositories, and stay updated on best practices through platforms like . Regularly review your application’s performance. Adjust configurations to minimize cold start delays and balance costs. Explore our recommended resources, including curated articles and community discussions, to stay ahead of the curve in Python API development.info: "Remember, every deployment is a learning opportunity. The key is to keep experimenting, measuring, and improving."The future of Python APIs may well be a blend of serverless and traditional hosting models. Your choice should depend on your project’s unique demands, your team's expertise, and your growth plans. Embrace the benefits of serverless where it makes sense, and don’t shy away from traditional approaches when they offer more control and predictability.Your journey toward a more efficient, modern backend starts today. Take action—experiment with serverless deployments, monitor your progress, and continuously iterate on your solutions. Whether you lean into serverless for its scalability or choose traditional hosting for its stability, remember that the tools and resources you need are at your fingertips.Now is the time to break free from traditional constraints, innovate with serverless technology, and redefine what’s possible with Python APIs. Embrace the future, make informed decisions, and let your code lead the way to success!Get started today and explore the endless possibilities of Python development at python.0x3d.site.How Hackers and Spies Use the Same Psychological Tricks Against YouImagine walking into any room—knowing exactly how to read people, influence decisions, and stay ten steps ahead. What if you understood the same psychological tactics that spies, hackers, and elite intelligence agencies use to manipulate, persuade, and control? This 11-module, 53-topic masterclass gives you that You’ll learn: ✅ The secrets of persuasion & mind control—so no one can manipulate you again. ✅ Surveillance & counter-surveillance tactics—know when you're being watched & how to disappear. ✅ Cyber intelligence & hacking psychology—understand how data is stolen & how to protect yourself. ✅ Real-world espionage strategies—used in covert operations, business, and even everyday life. 💡 For just the price of a coffee, you're not just buying a course. You're buying a new way of thinking, a new level of awareness, and a mental edge that 99% of people will never have.

item.title: Python Operator Chaining… and why you still can’t trust ChatGPT
item.author: Ari Abramowitz
item.content:encoded:
A colleague of mine recently posted the following on a Teams channel which we’ve appropriately named “Something cool I learned today!”: will evaluate to (!)This is because the [Python] interpreter reads this as , which is the same as .The first problem with this, which I did not catch until later, is that does not in fact evaluate to . It raises an exception because is not iterable so the operator does not work:Having overlooked that issue, I was about to respond with this link giving more details about operator precedence in Python. However, after looking more closely at the explanation there, I noticed something else: and have the same precedence and have left-to-right associativity (they get processed left-to-right). Following that, I would have expected the example to evaluate to :But when I actually ran the code, it returned .: Hey, ChatGPT, what's going on here?: Simple. It’s operator precedence.: But don’t and have the same precedence?: Good point. This should evaluate to .: But it doesn’t.: Oooohhh. Riiiighht. It’s .This was a good reminder that ChatGPT is still not a Python expert. I would have expected it to catch both of the points I mentioned above, unravel the incorrect assumptions, and then give the correct explanation. Instead, it gave a misleading answer and only corrected itself after two rounds of prodding. Oh well.Essentially, because of "operator chaining" the example above, , translates to the following:Since is not , the right half of the expression, and thereby all of it, evaluates to .This is similar to how Python evaluates as . Alternatively, using the example from the Python docs: is equivalent to I had known about this rule generally, but did not realize it applied in a case like this.If two or more comparison operators are "chained" together, Python inserts s between them to interpret what's going on.
"Comparison operators" include: , , , , , , , , , and ChatGPT is not a Python expert... yet.For more on this, check out these links:

item.title: Python Imports and Module Caching: Behind the Scenes
item.author: Ari Abramowitz
item.content:encoded:
What happens behind the scenes in a python statement? Glad I asked. Here's how it works: Python first checks to see if your module has already been loaded.
is a regular python that is used to cache modules. The keys are strings of the modules' names and the values are module objects (i.e. objects of type )If your module is already in , python adds the cached version to and skips the steps below. If python does not find the module in , it creates a new object and loads the module's source code into it The new objects is added to the cache Python compiles and the source code from your module Python and adds your module to , making it available for use going forward.: It is often said that importing a python module will execute all of the code in the module. That is only partly true. Python only executes a module the first time it is imported, then caches it in . Subsequent statements throughout your code, even in different files, will refer back to the cached version and will not execute the module again.Below is a bit of code to bring all of this home. We will:Create a simple module () with a print statement so we can see when the module gets executed.Import into , which will execute .Delete from , then try accessing it to demonstrate that it is no longer available.Re-import . This will add back to , but will use the cached version from and will not re-execute .Access again to demonstrate that is has in fact been added back to ======== Running main.py ======== Importing module1 Running module1 Deleting module1 Accessing module 1 after deleting from globals() NameError name 'module1' is not defined Re-importing module1 Accessing module 1 after re-importing from globals() Done! =================================

item.title: Read license plate with OCR
item.author: Jeremy
item.content:encoded:
So close! We now have cropped images with just license plate numbers. This is derived from highway-view, down to car-view, down to license plate. In this step, we pre-process the image to improve OCR, and we read the characters from image.The following pre-processing steps helps focus OCR on just the important image information, and reduces everything else (eg RBG color, grays, small contours). For clarity this sequence of pre-processing prior to OCR is fairly typical pattern prior to OCR taught in computer vision. I’m not inventing or discovering something new here. At the bottom of article, we run OCR with no pre-processing to illustrate the difference. Before pre-processing - cropped image of license plate from prior steps:Pre-process step: desaturate colors. Go gray.RGB color is not helpful here for OCR. Doesn’t matter if license plate numbers are magenta or cyan. Thus discard color information.Pre-process step: gaussian blur to reduce noise / artifacts slightly.Nevermind the “gaussian” part. This is just applying a blur filter to the image. That blur filter softens the small differences. And because small differences are reduced (eg dirty-ish license plate), then the big differences are easier to distinguish (eg dark-numbers on white-background).Pre-process step: threshold to really highlight license plate charactersThresholding further highlights where we have big differences such as dark-numbers on white-background. I experimented with a bunch of OCR libraries here. I found best for this use case was PaddleOCR, better than Tesseract and some others. Run PaddleOCR on thresholded image, and get string text, license plate number, back from the image![[[[14.0, 29.0], [181.0, 22.0], [184.0, 76.0], [17.0, 83.0]], ('CEZ2594', 0.9654235243797302)]]We read the license plate! Number to the right of string is confidence ie ~96.5% confident string is correct. And the left side numbers is bounding box coordinates. 🥳Next Link: Tie it all together: End-to-end License Plate Detection
Appendix: Interesting Points Folks might wonder: why not just OCR the original image? You can. But this is what you get:Note the right hand side not the red bounding box. Firstly, three of seven characters are wrong. This is the same input we just demonstrated, same license plate same image, only without pre-processing steps. Secondly, confidence score is low. This is why the pre-processing steps.

item.title: AWK Basics Tutorial
item.author: Leapcell
item.content:encoded:
AWK is a built-in text processing tool in Linux systems, specializing in handling structured text (e.g., logs, CSV files). It reads files line by line, processes data by fields, and supports simple programming logic.options] filename demo.txt
| : Total number of fields in current line | | /etc/passwd Input field separator (default space)Output field separator (default space)awk -v OFS=, '{print $1,$2}'awk '{print FILENAME}' file /etc/passwd
/etc/passwd
/etc/passwd awk '{print toupper($1)}'awk '{print substr($1,3,5)}'awk '{print int(rand()*100)}'
1. Single-line Conditions file
/etc/passwd /etc/passwd : Use instead of /etc/passwd : Memory-friendly line-by-line processing: Combine with /Hierarchical heading structureVariable/function tables for clarityCode block/output result contrastPractical tips and quick reference addedLearning curve enhanced through logical orderingImproved readability with proper spacing and indentationFinally, I recommend the best platform for deployment:

item.title: sqluv - simple terminal UI for DBMS & local CSV/TSV/LTSV
item.author: nchika
item.content:encoded:
I previously released the sqly command. sqly is a tool that allows you to execute SQL on CSV, TSV, LTSV, JSON, and Excel files, and it provided a custom shell to enhance user-friendliness. I have compiled articles about sqly here.When expanding sqly's features, I reconsidered the interface and realized that a simpler Text User Interface (TUI) would make it more intuitive. As I envisioned a TUI, I came up with the idea that "it would be even more useful if the same UI could connect to a DBMS."This thought process led me to develop the sqluv command. The name comes from SQL + LOVE. I chose this name with the hope that sqluv would help people become more familiar with SQL.
Execution Example: Running SQL on CSV/TSV/LTSV When sqluv receives file paths (multiple files supported) as startup arguments, it automatically loads the files and stores their data in an in-memory SQLite3 database. The table names are derived from the filenames by removing their extensions (e.g., if the file is , the table name will be ).Due to the nature of writing data into SQLite3, executing an UPDATE statement in SQL does not modify the original local files. In the original sqly, there was a feature to reflect UPDATE results back into the local files, so I plan to port this feature to sqluv at a later time.
Database Connection Feature If no file paths are specified at startup, sqluv enters DBMS mode.New: Enter new database connection details and connect.List: Select from previously saved connection details and connect.The New Database Connection screen, which appears after pressing the New button, allows users to input connection details. If the connection is successful, the information is saved in . The Saved Connections screen, which appears after pressing the List button, displays a list of previously entered connection names. Users can navigate the list using the arrow keys and press Enter to connect.Currently, there is no feature to manually delete connection entries. However, if a connection attempt fails, a modal will appear asking whether to delete the failed connection entry.The HOME screen consists of:Sidebar: Displays the list of tables.Top-right area: SQL input field.Bottom-right area: SQL execution result display.Press Ctrl-D or ESC to exit the application, and use TAB or Shift-TAB to switch focus between elements.After entering an SQL query and pressing the Execute Query button, the table data is displayed. Users can navigate and scroll through the table using the arrow keys or mouse.Initially, I planned to execute SQL queries with Ctrl-Enter, but due to technical limitations where Ctrl-Enter was mistakenly recognized as a regular Enter, I had to reluctantly introduce the Execute Query button instead.sqluv is a newly developed command, and I expect there may still be some bugs. Moving forward, my goals are to fix bugs and provide a more user-friendly TUI.To keep my development motivation high, I’d really appreciate it if you could press the GitHub Star button! Even better, if you’re willing to sponsor me, that would be amazing (I was recently surprised to receive my very first sponsor!). Bug reports are also more than welcome.Thank you all for reading until the end!
